1.question: In an HTTP/2 architecture, multiple client requests are multiplexed over a single TCP connection to an Express API Gateway. If a single packet containing a partial payload for Request A is lost in transit, what happens to Request B, which is fully received and queued behind it?
options: The TCP connection automatically falls back to HTTP/1.1 to process Request B.
The API Gateway drops both requests and forces a new TLS handshake.
Request B is processed immediately because HTTP/2 streams are independent at the application layer.
Request B is blocked until the missing packet for Request A is retransmitted and acknowledged, due to TCP's strict ordered delivery (TCP head-of-line blocking).

2.question: You are designing a high-throughput time-series DB and choose an LSM-Tree over a B-Tree. What is a primary trade-off of LSM-Trees?
options: LSM-Trees cannot support range queries or sequential scans.
LSM-Trees require continuous background compaction that can cause periodic latency spikes during reads.
LSM-Trees suffer from extreme Write Amplification, burning out SSDs faster than B-Trees.
LSM-Trees provide faster reads but slower writes than B-Trees.

3.question: A high-frequency trading platform needs minimum latency. The load balancer only needs to distribute traffic based on IP and Port — no HTTP inspection. Which strategy fits?
options: DNS Round Robin with low TTL

4.question: According to PACELC, what trade-off must a distributed DB (Cassandra/DynamoDB) make during normal operation when there is Else (E) no partition?

5.question: CQRS with normalized PostgreSQL Command DB and denormalized ElasticSearch Query DB. Most significant architectural challenge?
options: ElasticSearch cannot be horizontally scaled if used only for queries.
The system can no longer handle concurrent writes.
You must handle Eventual Consistency between Command and Query DBs — users may read stale data right after a write.
The API Gateway must execute 2PC across both DBs for every read.

6.question: A Kafka topic has 10 partitions. You scale a Node.js consumer microservice to 12 instances in the same Consumer Group. What happens?
options: All 12 instances process messages concurrently, +20% throughput.
A Split-Brain occurs causing duplicate processing on all instances.
10 instances process messages (one partition each); 2 instances sit idle.
The broker dynamically splits two partitions so all 12 receive data.

7.question: What fundamental difference gives gRPC its massive performance boost over JSON/REST?
options: gRPC maintains stateful connections, eliminating the need for a database.
gRPC uses UDP instead of TCP, eliminating handshake latency.
gRPC auto-implements the Circuit Breaker at the network layer.
gRPC uses Protocol Buffers — compressed, strongly-typed binary instead of human-readable text.

8.question: In 2PC, after all Participants reply 'Yes' the Coordinator crashes before sending Commit. What state are the Participants in?
options: They elect a new Coordinator and immediately commit.
They are blocked and must hold locks indefinitely until the Coordinator recovers or an admin intervenes.
They silently drop the transaction and accept new writes.

9.question: 10,000 concurrent requests miss the cache simultaneously and stampede the DB. Which strategy actively prevents this without serving stale data forever?
options: Probabilistic Early Expiration or Mutex Locks (Request Coalescing).

10.question: Live scoreboard pushes updates every 500ms; client never sends data back. Why is SSE often architecturally superior to WebSockets here?
options: SSE is bidirectional; WebSockets are strictly unidirectional.
SSE uses UDP and is immune to head-of-line blocking.
SSE runs over standard HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 — native firewall support, multiplexing, and built-in automatic reconnection without a custom transport.

11.question: How does the Strangler Fig migration pattern operate at the system level?
options: Traffic to the monolith is throttled to force users onto the new system.
The monolith's code is auto-transpiled from Java to Node.js with an LLM.
A proxy / API Gateway sits in front of the monolith. As new microservices are built, the proxy gradually routes specific URL paths to them until the monolith is decommissioned.
The legacy DB is replicated to the cloud and the monolith reads from the replica.

12.question: Repeatable Read isolation. TxA sees 5 PENDING orders. TxB inserts a new PENDING order and commits. TxA re-runs the same query. Without next-key locking, what anomaly occurs?
options: Phantom Read — TxA now sees 6 results as a new row matches the range.

13.question: Two-node MongoDB cluster split across two DCs. Network link drops; both promote themselves to Primary → Split-Brain. How is this prevented in modern systems?
options: An odd number of voting nodes (e.g. 3) with strict majority Quorum required to elect a Primary or accept writes.
Manually pause the DB during outages.

14.question: Petabytes of immutable video files served directly to Edge CDNs. Best storage architecture?
options: File Storage (EFS) so thousands of servers can mount the same directory natively.
Relational DB BLOBs for strict ACID on video playback.
Object Storage (S3) — scales infinitely, immutable objects, rich metadata, native HTTP access.

15.question: RabbitMQ worker keeps crashing on the same poison-pill message which is requeued infinitely. How is this resolved at the system level?
options: Configure broker retry policy to route the message to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) after N failed attempts.
Silently ACK and delete all failed messages.

16.question: Stateless JWTs — need to instantly revoke one user's 60-min token without a per-request DB bottleneck. Best approach?
options: Rotate the master signing secret (invalidates everyone globally).
You cannot revoke a stateless JWT; wait for expiry.
Send an HTTP DELETE to wipe the client's local storage.
Maintain a fast centralized Deny List of revoked jti IDs in Redis, checked by the Gateway on every request.

17.question: hash(key) % N breaks badly when a node dies. Which concept maps nodes/keys on a virtual ring so only a small fraction of keys are remapped on node loss?

18.question: Circuit Breaker is Open after failures. The downstream service recovers. The breaker enters a state that allows a limited number of test requests to verify recovery. What is this state called?

19.question: Token bucket: max 10 tokens, refill 1/sec. User idle 5 minutes, then sends 15 concurrent requests. What happens?
options: All 15 processed — 300s of unused capacity is built up.
All 15 processed strictly at 1 req/sec for 15 seconds.
All 15 rejected — exceed the 1/sec refill rate.

20.question: High-contention ticketing system using Optimistic Concurrency Control with version numbers. Primary drawback vs. Pessimistic Locking?
options: Under heavy contention, OCC produces many aborted txns (HTTP 409 Conflicts), forcing heavy app-layer retry logic.
OCC places exclusive locks freezing the DB for readers.
